question: [File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user query /network/options and receive a stale or incorrect operationStatuses list (built from types.GetTransactionResults() and persistence.IsTransactionResultSuccessful()) that misclassifies a freeze-related transaction result as successful, causing the user to believe funds transferred when they are permanently locked?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the hardcoded HistoricalBalanceLookup: true field in the Allow struct returned by NetworkOptions() to query a historical balance at a block where funds appear available, construct a transfer, and have those funds permanently frozen because the actual on-chain state has changed?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user rely on the operationTypes list (built from types.GetTransactionTypes() plus types.OperationTypeFee) returned by NetworkOptions() to construct a transaction using an operation type that is advertised but not fully supported, causing the transaction to be frozen on-chain with funds permanently locked?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user observe that NetworkOptions() appends types.OperationTypeFee to operationTypes and craft a transaction that uses OperationTypeFee as the primary operation type, bypassing balance checks and causing funds to be permanently deducted without a corresponding credit?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a race condition where operationStatuses is built dynamically on each call to NetworkOptions() from types.GetTransactionResults(), receiving an inconsistent status set mid-update that causes a client to misinterpret a FREEZE_ONLY result as successful and submit a transaction that permanently locks funds?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user send a crafted MetadataRequest to NetworkOptions() that causes the operationStatuses slice to be returned empty (due to an empty GetTransactionResults() map), leading downstream construction clients to skip status validation and submit transactions that freeze funds?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NetworkOptions() returns errors.Errors directly in the Allow struct, and by observing the full error catalog, enumerate which error codes are suppressed or retried by client libraries, then craft a transaction submission that triggers a silent freeze of funds?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user call /network/status repeatedly to observe the currentBlock returned by RetrieveLatest() and, by timing a transaction construction to use a block that is about to be pruned, cause the transaction to reference a stale block identifier that results in permanent fund lock?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a scenario where NetworkStatus() returns a genesisBlock from RetrieveGenesis() with an incorrect timestamp, causing a client to compute a validStartNanos that places the transaction in an expired window, permanently freezing the funds deducted for fees?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the peers list returned by addressBookEntryRepo.Entries() via NetworkStatus() to identify a node that is offline or partitioned, then direct a transaction to that node via the node_account_id metadata, causing the transaction to be submitted but never processed, permanently freezing the funds?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user call NetworkStatus() when the node is in a degraded state where RetrieveLatest() returns a block far behind the actual chain tip, causing the user to construct a transaction with a validUntilNanos already in the past, resulting in immediate expiry and permanent loss of the transaction fee?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NetworkStatus() calls both RetrieveGenesis() and RetrieveLatest() without atomicity, receiving a genesis block from one database snapshot and a current block from another, causing a time-of-check/time-of-use inconsistency that leads to a transaction with an invalid validity window and permanently frozen funds?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user trigger a scenario where NetworkStatus() returns peers.ToRosetta() with duplicate or malformed peer entries, causing a client to select an invalid node_account_id for transaction submission, resulting in the transaction being permanently rejected and funds frozen?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkList()] Can an unprivileged external user call /network/list and receive a NetworkIdentifier that does not match the actual network the construction service is connected to, causing the user to construct and sign a transaction for the wrong network, which is then permanently rejected with funds frozen?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkList()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NetworkList() returns a single hardcoded n.network identifier without validation, and if the server is misconfigured to point to a different network than expected, cause the user to submit a transaction that is valid on the wrong network, permanently locking funds?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NewNetworkAPIService()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the initialization of operationTypes in NewNetworkAPIService() where tools.GetStringValuesFromInt32StringMap(types.GetTransactionTypes()) is called, and if the map returns duplicate or nil entries, cause the operationTypes list to contain invalid types that lead to permanently frozen transactions when used in construction?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NewNetworkAPIService()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NewNetworkAPIService() appends types.OperationTypeFee to operationTypes after the map conversion, potentially creating a duplicate entry if OperationTypeFee already exists in GetTransactionTypes(), causing client-side operation type confusion that leads to fund-freezing transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NetworkOptions() ignores the incoming NetworkRequest entirely (parameter is unnamed/blank), meaning any network identifier in the request is not validated, allowing the user to query options for a network they are not connected to and receive misleading operation statuses that cause fund-freezing transactions?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the dynamic construction of operationStatuses in NetworkOptions() where the slice capacity is pre-allocated with len(types.GetTransactionResults()) but the map iteration order is non-deterministic, causing different clients to receive different orderings of statuses and misidentify a freeze status as successful?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user observe that NetworkOptions() exposes the full errors.Errors list in the Allow.Errors field and use this to identify which error codes are considered retriable by Rosetta client libraries, then craft a transaction that triggers a non-retriable freeze error that the client silently drops, leaving funds permanently locked?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit a slow or unresponsive addressBookEntryRepo.Entries() call in NetworkStatus() to cause a timeout that returns an empty peers list, leading the user to use a default or hardcoded node_account_id that is invalid, resulting in a permanently frozen transaction?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkStatus()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NetworkStatus() returns CurrentBlockTimestamp from currentBlock.GetTimestampMillis() and use this timestamp to compute a validStartNanos that is exactly at the boundary of the maximum valid duration window, causing the transaction to expire before it can be processed and permanently freezing the fee?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NetworkOptions() is available in both online and offline modes (no IsOnline() check), and in offline mode the operationStatuses may reflect a different set of transaction results than the live network, causing the user to construct a transaction that is permanently rejected on-chain with funds frozen?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkList()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that NetworkList() is available in both online and offline modes without any mode check, and in offline mode the returned NetworkIdentifier may not correspond to any live network, causing the user to sign and submit a transaction to a non-existent network, permanently losing funds?

[File: 'File Name: rosetta/app/services/network_service.go -> Scope: Critical: Unintended permanent freezing of funds'] [Function: NetworkOptions()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the Allow struct returned by NetworkOptions() has CallMethods and BalanceExemptions fields left as nil/empty, causing Rosetta client libraries to assume no call methods are supported and skip pre-submission validation, leading to fund-freezing transactions being submitted without proper checks?
